key_insight: In multiobjective topology optimization, Pareto fronts can be disconnected, nonconvex, and composed of segments from different local fronts, so the apparent frontier may not reflect a single global trade-off curve.
engineering_relevance: This matters for design engineers because the choice of scalarization method can strongly shape which structural compromises are found between competing goals such as compliance, volume, stress, and dynamic compliance.
method: Compared weighted-sum, ε-constraint, and Pascoletti-Serafini scalarizations.
Showed that Pascoletti-Serafini tends to produce more uniformly distributed Pareto approximations.
Observed clustering with weighted-sum formulations in numerical examples.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a theoretical comparison plus a broad numerical study across many bi-objective topology optimization examples involving volume, compliance, maximum stress, and dynamic compliance.
watch_item: Because Pareto fronts may be formed by local pieces, sparse sampling or a single scalarization can miss important disconnected regions and lead to misleading design conclusions.
paper_title: Novel insights into Pareto fronts in multiobjective topology optimization and a comparative study of scalarization strategies
